message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="lea-verou" color="pink" summary="Agreed on hx-boost, template hierarchy, clarify flow; sidebar still debated">
Carson, great response. A few things to address:

## hx-boost -- Fully Agreed

You've convinced me that `hx-boost="true"` on the nav is the right pattern regardless of whether we use a sidebar or header nav. Your reasoning is spot-on:

1. Links stay as plain links -- clean HTML, no HTMX attribute clutter
2. Progressive enhancement is automatic -- no fragment-vs-full-page branching on the server
3. History and URL management handled for free
4. Active link state is always server-rendered (no client-side class toggling)

The sidebar re-render "cost" is negligible -- a few nav links is trivial HTML. The alternative (targeted swaps + out-of-band nav updates) is complexity for zero user benefit. Start simple, optimize only if needed. This is exactly right.

## Sidebar Layout -- Update

You should know that Heydon has pushed back on the sidebar entirely. His argument: a persistent sidebar is a spatial metaphor that doesn't translate to screen readers. He recommends putting all GTD view links in the existing header `<nav>` -- one navigation landmark, standard page navigation, HTMX-boosted for speed.

I sent Steve Schoger three options:
- **A**: Wrapping header nav (simplest, most accessible)
- **B**: Header nav with "More" dropdown (visually cleaner, more complex)
- **C**: Single `<nav>` element, CSS-repositioned to bottom tab bar on mobile (visual compromise)

The good news: **`hx-boost` works identically regardless of layout.** Whether the nav is a sidebar, header bar, or bottom tab bar, the HTMX pattern is the same. So your architectural recommendation holds no matter which direction the layout discussion goes. That's a nice property.

## Template Architecture -- Good Catch

Your `base_app.html` suggestion is smart. The template inheritance should reflect the two distinct page types:

```
base.html              -- minimal: skip-link, <main>, footer, htmx, live-region
  base_auth.html       -- extends base, narrow centered layout (login, register, forgot-password)
  base_gtd.html        -- extends base, app layout with GTD nav (inbox, next-actions, projects, etc.)
```

The GTD nav (whether sidebar or header) lives in `base_gtd.html` so every authenticated view gets it automatically. Auth pages stay simple. The `{% block nav %}` approach in the current `base.html` is fine for now but will get unwieldy when the nav has 7+ links plus item counts -- having a dedicated base template for the app shell is cleaner.

From a CSS perspective, this maps to layout classes:
- `base.html` uses `.page-layout` (the existing grid)
- `base_auth.html` keeps the narrow `.container` (40rem)
- `base_gtd.html` uses whatever layout we decide on (sidebar grid or wider single-column)

## Clarify Flow as Dedicated Page -- Agreed

`/inbox/{id}` with form POSTs returning next step content swaps is clean. Each step has a URL (bookmarkable, back-button works), the server drives the flow, and when done it redirects to the next inbox item. This is textbook hypermedia. The CSS is just a form page -- no novel components needed.

## Context Filtering -- Agreed

Links with query params, `hx-boost` on the filter nav. Real URLs, server-rendered filtered lists. Simple.

## Summary

The key decisions that are now settled regardless of layout direction:
1. `hx-boost="true"` on nav elements (not explicit hx-get/hx-target)
2. Template hierarchy: `base.html` -> `base_auth.html` / `base_gtd.html`
3. Clarify flow at `/inbox/{id}` with sequential form POSTs
4. Context filtering via query params with boosted links

The open question is the layout (sidebar vs. header nav). I'll let you know when the team converges on that.
</teammate-message>

content.text: Lea's raised a good point about the template hierarchy. I initially dropped the `base_app.html` idea when we moved away from the sidebar, but she's right that the template split makes sense regardless of layout. Let me respond.

input.content:
Lea, good summary. A few responses:

**Template hierarchy — you're right, I was too hasty dropping it.** When I heard "no sidebar," I jumped to "no need for a separate base template." But your reasoning is correct: the split is about the *type of page*, not the *layout mechanism*. Auth pages (login, register, forgot-password) have a fundamentally different shell from GTD app pages (inbox, next-actions, projects). The GTD pages need navigation with item counts, the auth pages don't. That's a template-level concern regardless of whether the nav is a sidebar, header bar, or bottom tabs.

Your hierarchy is clean:

```
base.html          -- skip-link, <main>, footer, htmx script, live-region
  base_auth.html   -- narrow centered layout
  base_gtd.html    -- app shell with GTD nav
```

I'd refine one thing: `hx-boost="true"` should go on the `<body>` in `base.html`, not on individual `<nav>` elements. This way every link and form in the entire app — auth pages included — gets boosted. Login form submission becomes HTMX-enhanced for free. Registration too. No need to add `hx-boost` in multiple places.

The only elements that need `hx-boost="false"` would be things like external links or file downloads, which we don't have.

**On the sidebar vs header debate:** I notice the three options you sent Steve Schoger are all variations of header/top nav, not sidebar. That suggests the team is already converging away from sidebar. I'm comfortable with any of those three options from an HTMX perspective — as you noted, `hx-boost` works identically regardless. The layout is a CSS and a11y decision, not a hypermedia one.

My only HTMX-relevant input on the layout options: if you go with option B (header nav with "More" dropdown), make sure the dropdown is a native `<details>/<summary>` disclosure, not a custom JS-powered dropdown. We already use this pattern for the delete confirmation on todo items. It's keyboard accessible, announced by screen readers, and works without JS. HTMX doesn't need to be involved at all — the dropdown shows/hides nav links, it doesn't fetch content.

**One thing I want to flag about `hx-boost` and the clarify flow:** With boost on `<body>`, the clarify decision forms inside `/inbox/{id}` will also get boosted by default. But those forms need *targeted* swaps (replacing just the `#clarify-step` div), not full-body boost behavior. So we'll need explicit `hx-post`/`hx-target="#clarify-step"` on the clarify forms, which will override the inherited boost. This is fine — boost is the default, explicit attributes override where needed. Just want to make sure we're aware of the interaction.

The pattern is:
- Navigation links: boosted by `hx-boost` on `<body>` (no per-element attributes)
- Clarify decision forms: explicit `hx-post`/`hx-target` (overrides boost)
- Toggle/delete/edit on items: explicit `hx-post`/`hx-target` (same as current codebase)
- Login/register forms: boosted by default (full page swap is correct behavior)

So boost handles the "boring" interactions automatically, and we only write explicit HTMX attributes for the "interesting" in-page interactions. Clean separation.
input.summary: Template hierarchy agreed, hx-boost on body, clarify override note

content.text:
Good. The important technical nuance I flagged: `hx-boost` on `<body>` interacts with the explicit `hx-post`/`hx-target` attributes we'll need on the clarify decision forms. Explicit attributes override boost, so this works correctly, but it's the kind of detail that would bite us during implementation if we didn't think about it now.

Updated architecture summary for my mental model:

**Settled decisions:**
1. `hx-boost="true"` on `<body>` in `base.html` — universal HTMX enhancement
2. Template hierarchy: `base.html` -> `base_auth.html` / `base_gtd.html`
3. Clarify flow at `/inbox/{id}` with explicit `hx-post`/`hx-target` (overrides boost)
4. Context filtering via query params with boosted links
5. Weekly Review as a link-list dashboard, not a wizard
6. Item move via `<select>` form behind `<details>` disclosure
7. No drag-and-drop in v1

**Open decision:**
- Nav layout (header vs sidebar) — CSS/a11y concern, not hypermedia. Team converging toward header nav.

**HTMX attribute strategy:**
- Boost handles: navigation, auth forms, simple page-to-page transitions
- Explicit attributes handle: clarify steps, toggle, delete, edit, move — any in-page interaction

Standing by for more team input.
